title: Searching Inside Files
subtitle: Find text with grep

project.readme.txt:
This is my project.
It runs on port 3000.
See config.txt for settings.

sections.content: Real files can have hundreds of lines. Instead of reading everything, `grep` lets you **search** for specific text and shows only the matching lines.
sections.analogy: It's like using Ctrl+F (Find) in a document — type what you're looking for, and only the matches light up.
keyPoints: `grep` searches for a pattern inside a file
It prints only the lines that match

sections.instruction: `grep` searches for text inside files. It shows every line that contains your search term.
sections.prompt: Type `grep "port" app.js` to find every line mentioning "port".

onSuccess.message: Two lines matched. `grep` shows every line containing your search text.

sections.instruction: Try searching for something else. How many `console.log` calls are in the file?
sections.prompt: Type `grep "console" app.js` to find all console.log lines.

onSuccess.message: Three console.log lines found. This is how developers find specific code in files.

sections.instruction: Developers often leave TODO comments as reminders. Let's find them.
sections.prompt: Type `grep "TODO" app.js` to search for TODO comments.

onSuccess.message: Found the TODO comment. In real projects, `grep` helps you track down notes and issues across hundreds of files.

sections.instruction: What if you're not sure about capitalization? The `-i` flag makes grep ignore case.

onSuccess.message: Same result! `-i` ignores case, so "todo", "TODO", and "Todo" all match. Very useful when you're not sure how something was written.

completionMessage: `grep` is one of the most powerful terminal tools. It searches for text patterns inside files. Add `-i` to ignore case. In later lessons, you'll learn to search across multiple files and use patterns to find even more.
